title: Treacle tart, the classic
subtitle: Shortcrust case, golden syrup and breadcrumb filling
excerpt: The British tart made with golden syrup and fresh breadcrumbs in a shortcrust case. Sharp lemon juice cuts the sweetness. Serve in thin slices with double cream or clotted cream.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: golden syrup tart variants with crumb and pastry. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): treacle tart as a regional British staple. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: sweet pastry and syrup-based tarts. Golden syrup was first produced commercially in Britain in 1883 (Abram Lyle); the modern treacle tart using Lyle's is a post-1883 recipe, though the combination of syrup filling in a shortcrust case predates that in recipes using other thick syrups.

content.text: Treacle tart has four components: shortcrust pastry, golden syrup, breadcrumbs, and lemon. The proportions matter. Too few breadcrumbs and the filling stays liquid; too many and it bakes solid and dry. The lemon is not optional; without it the tart is sugar all the way down. This recipe uses a ratio of roughly four parts syrup to one part fresh breadcrumbs, which gives a set that is firm enough to slice cleanly but still yielding in the centre.

The pastry is blind-baked before the filling goes in. Without that step the base stays raw and the filling soaks through. The syrup is warmed slightly before mixing with the breadcrumbs so it flows through them rather than pooling at the edges.
What you need

items.name: Golden syrup
items.prepNote: about 12 tablespoons (the tin lid is a useful measure)

items.name: Bread
items.prepNote: white bread, blitzed to fine fresh breadcrumbs (about 75 g)

content.text: The filling uses fresh white breadcrumbs, not dried. Dried breadcrumbs absorb too much liquid and give a tight, dense filling. Blitz two or three slices of slightly stale white bread in a food processor to get soft, fine crumbs.
Method
Make the shortcrust

Weigh {{plain-flour}} plain flour and {{sea-salt-fine}} a pinch of sea salt into the bowl. Add {{unsalted-butter}} cold butter cubes and rub the butter into the flour between your fingertips, lifting the mixture as you work to keep it cool. Stop when the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs with some pea-sized butter pieces remaining; those larger pieces make the baked pastry shorter and crumblier.

Add the {{water}} cold water a tablespoon at a time, mixing with a knife between additions. Stop as soon as the dough comes together, 3 to 4 tablespoons is usually enough. Flatten the dough into a disc, wrap in cling film, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.
Line the tin

On a lightly floured surface, roll the chilled dough to about 3 mm thick. Lift it over the rolling pin and lower it into the 23 cm tart tin, pressing gently into the fluted edges without stretching it. Run the rolling pin over the top of the tin to trim the overhang cleanly. Prick the base all over with a fork. Refrigerate for another 10 minutes; cold pastry shrinks less in the oven.
Blind bake the case

Heat the oven to 190°C fan. Line the pastry case with a sheet of crumpled baking paper, then fill it with baking beans, at least 400 g so the paper comes up the sides and holds them in. The weight keeps the sides upright during the initial bake. This is the
blind baking

stage. Bake for 18 minutes, until the pastry sides are set. Remove the paper and beans and bake for another 5 minutes until the base is pale gold and dry. The edges should be matte and just beginning to colour.
Make the filling and bake the tart

Gently warm {{golden-syrup}} golden syrup in a saucepan over low heat until it loosens and flows easily, 2 to 3 minutes; it shouldn't boil. Stir in the breadcrumbs, the juice and finely grated zest of one lemon. The filling should look thick and barely pourable. Pour it into the hot blind-baked case and spread to within 5 mm of the pastry edge. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes; the filling is done when it is set across the whole surface with no visible wobble and is deep golden-brown at the edges, paler gold at the centre.
Cool and serve

Leave the tart in the tin for 20 minutes before attempting to remove it; the filling is molten when it comes out of the oven and needs time to firm up. Serve in thin slices at room temperature or barely warm, with cold double cream or clotted cream.
Troubleshooting

attrs.intro: The three problems most people encounter with treacle tart.

items.fix: Check the weight of your golden syrup and breadcrumbs carefully. Bake until the top shows no wobble when the tin is nudged; a slightly darker surface is better than an underbaked centre.
items.cause: Too few breadcrumbs relative to the syrup, or the tart was underbaked.
items.symptom: Filling doesn't set; liquid under the surface after cooling

items.fix: After removing the baking beans, give the base 5 to 7 minutes in the oven rather than 5. The colour cue: pale gold, not white. If the base is white it is not dry enough.
items.cause: Blind-bake step too short, the base didn't dry out fully before the filling went in.
items.symptom: Pastry base is soft and slightly doughy

items.fix: Use the full juice and zest of one lemon. The acid is the counterpoint to the syrup; without it the tart is one-dimensional.
items.symptom: Filling too sweet, cloying to eat in portions

attrs.heading: When things go sideways

content.text: Where this tart sits

Treacle tart as we know it is a post-1883 recipe, after Abram Lyle's golden syrup became widely available in Britain. Before golden syrup, similar tarts used black treacle, honey, or reduced fruit syrups. The breadcrumb-syrup filling is common to most versions of the recipe recorded between 1900 and 1940 in British household cookbooks. Florence White's 1932 collection documents it as a staple of the traditional household sweet course.
